text: The electrochemical reduction of CO into value-added liquid fuels and chemicals is considered to be one of the most promising carbon recycling strategies. However, efficient catalysts that exhibit high activity at low overpotentials remain to be developed. Recently, oxide-derived Cu (OD-Cu) was confirmed to be an exceptionally active catalyst for CO reduction reaction (CORR). Unfortunately, the active sites that account for such high activity of OD-Cu are not clearly identified. To address this issue, a temperature-programmed desorption (TPD) experiment of CO as well as electron nanodiffraction (END) were employed to study the surface chemistry and structure of OD-Cu. A distinct peak was observed at high temperature (up to 573 K) on OD-Cu catalysts in TPD experiments, which is totally absent on the polycrystalline Cu foils. Moreover, the existence of this feature was also confirmed to be largely dependent on Cu oxide precursors and its thermal annealing conditions. As shown in Fig. 7, when Cu oxide precursors are varied by increasing annealing temperature from 300 to 400 to 500 °C, the activity on OD-Cu catalysts was found to decrease sharply with increases of 28, 36 and up to 44 times, which are attributed to not only reduced number of GB terminations but also reduction in adsorption strength of CO. Therefore, our results showed that metastable surface defects associated with grain boundary terminations are the key active sites for such high selectivity and activity toward C2 products at OD-Cu catalysts.
